results.possibleIdentity: Graydon Cairl (born approximately 1928) was an engineer and technical professional active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, noted for his collaboration with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. He served as a technical collaborator for artist Sheila Berkley on a project featured in the landmark E.A.T. exhibition, 'Some More Beginnings: An Exhibition of Submitted Works Involving Technical Materials and Processes,' held at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) from November 1968 to February 1969. Professionally, Cairl was associated with the National Transparent Manufacturing Company Incorporated, based in Neshanic Station, New Jersey, where his expertise focused on thermal setting plastics and flexible plastics. A resident of Neshanic Station, he was married to the artist Joyce Cairl (m. 1959) and graduated from Norristown Area High School in 1947. His work in the late 1960s exemplified the E.A.T. mission of pairing artists with industrial engineers to explore new materials and technologies in creative practice.
